name: Doc Cook
abstract: Charles L. Cooke (September 3, 1891 – December 25, 1958), known as Doc Cook, was an American jazz bandleader and arranger.

about_quotes.speaker: Ernest "Punch" Miller
about_quotes.evidence: Did you work with Doc Cook? Yes sir. ... But Doc Cook played in the same place and I worked with him at the Dream Land.

about_quotes.speaker: Zilner T. Randolph
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER mentions Doc Cook as a leader with resistance to cabaret musicians; INTERVIEWEE does not mention Doc Cook and states they were busy with their own situation.

about_quotes.speaker: Lillie Delk Christian
about_quotes.evidence: Well, I had met him. You know, he was a... My foster sister knew him, and he came to see her, you know. And she... He was... I was introduced to him.

about_quotes.speaker: Zutty Singleton
about_quotes.evidence: Well Doc Cook, he wanted us to join his band you know? He came out to listen to us and everything. And he liked the way I played and everything.

about_quotes.speaker: Danny Barker
about_quotes.evidence: Doc Cook is a famous musician who had a great band. Jimmie Noone and Freddie Keppard was in his band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Danny Barker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Amacker
about_quotes.evidence: Did you ever know Charlie Cook? Doc Cook? ... Yeah, oh God yeah. Cook, that's the greatest musician.
